comments.text: Though perhaps very obvious, it may be worth mentioning that the convolution identity $\phi=\mu\ast \text{id}$ yields\[\sum_{n\ge1}\frac{\phi(n)}{2^n}=\sum_{n\ge1}\frac{\mu(n)}{(2^n-1)^2}+\frac{1}{2}.\]

A small related special case is easy to handle. If one keeps only the terms with \(n=2^m\), one obtains\[ S_2:=\sum_{m\geq 1}\frac{\phi(2^m)}{2^{2^m}}. \]Since \(\phi(2^m)=2^{m-1}\), this becomes\[ S_2=\sum_{m\geq 1}\frac{2^{m-1}}{2^{2^m}} =\sum_{m\geq 1}2^{-(2^m-m+1)}. \]Thus the binary expansion of \(S_2\) has nonzero digits at positions\[ e_m=2^m-m+1. \]Moreover\[ e_{m+1}-e_m = \bigl(2^{m+1}-(m+1)+1\bigr)-\bigl(2^m-m+1\bigr) = 2^m-1, \]so the gaps between successive nonzero binary digits tend to infinity. If \(S_2\) were rational, its binary expansion would be eventually periodic. Since \(S_2\) has infinitely many nonzero binary digits, eventual periodicity would force the gaps between nonzero digits to be bounded. This contradicts \(e_{m+1}-e_m=2^m-1\to\infty\). Hence\[ \sum_{m\geq 1}\frac{\phi(2^m)}{2^{2^m}} \]is irrational. Of course, this does not prove the irrationality of \(\sum_{n\geq 1}\phi(n)/2^n\), since the omitted terms may create carries or cancellations. It only shows that the powers-of-two subseries already contains a simple lacunary irrational component.
